skill.name: azure-eventgrid-dotnet
skill.description: Guidance and examples for publishing, receiving, parsing, and handling Azure Event Grid events with the .NET SDK.
skill.summary: Build Azure Event Grid publishers and consumers with .NET SDK patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static findings are false positives caused by Markdown fences, C# attributes, placeholder Azure endpoints, and documentation links. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, hidden execution path, or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fence Backticks
low_findings.description: Static Ruby or shell backtick detections occur in Markdown code fences and inline SDK references. The examples show package installation and C# usage, not executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged tokens are Markdown delimiters or inline code formatting. The surrounding content is static documentation, with no script file or runtime execution path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Azure Functions Attribute
low_findings.description: The dynamic function constructor hit is the C# Azure Functions attribute [Function("ProcessCloudEvent")]. It names a serverless trigger method and does not construct or execute dynamic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line is a C# attribute in a fenced example block. It is not JavaScript Function construction or runtime code evaluation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Azure URLs and Reference Links
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs are placeholder Event Grid endpoints and official package, documentation, quickstart, and SDK source links. No URL receives secrets or user data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to Azure Event Grid placeholders and official Microsoft or NuGet resources. No outbound request code or exfiltration behavior appears in the skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Lexical Blocker Matches
low_findings.description: Weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts are lexical matches in frontmatter, headings, SDK names, event type names, and documentation text. No cryptographic implementation or host reconnaissance command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are descriptive Markdown and C# examples. They contain no MD5 or DES use, shell reconnaissance, environment enumeration, or system probing.

content.user_title: Build Azure Event Grid Apps in .NET
content.value_statement: Azure Event Grid integrations require correct SDK choices, authentication, schemas, and delivery handling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical .NET patterns for publishers, consumers, and Azure Functions.
actual_capabilities: Explains EventGridPublisherClient usage for topics and domains.
Shows CloudEvent and EventGridEvent publishing patterns.
Documents namespace pull delivery with sender and receiver clients.
Provides Azure Functions trigger examples for Event Grid events.
Covers parsing, acknowledgement, release, rejection, and failover patterns.
limitations: Does not provision Azure resources or configure Event Grid topics.
Does not include runnable project files or automated tests.
Uses illustrative endpoints and credentials that users must replace.
Does not cover every Azure system event type in detail.

use_cases.title: Create Event Publishers
use_cases.description: Use SDK examples to publish custom application events to Azure Event Grid topics or domains.

use_cases.title: Build Pull Consumers
use_cases.description: Follow receiver patterns for namespace subscriptions, event locking, acknowledgement, retry, and rejection flows.

use_cases.title: Implement Serverless Handlers
use_cases.description: Reference Azure Functions trigger examples for EventGridEvent and CloudEvent processing.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the SDK Choices
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain when I should use EventGridPublisherClient, EventGridSenderClient, and EventGridReceiverClient in a .NET Azure Event Grid app.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Publisher
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a .NET publisher that sends CloudEvents to an Azure Event Grid topic using managed identity where possible.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Consumer Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Event Grid namespace consumer design for acknowledgement, release, rejection, retry, and idempotent processing concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Resilient Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a production Azure Event Grid integration for a .NET service with CloudEvents, schema validation, failover, dead-letter handling, and Azure Functions consumers.

output_examples.input: Explain how to publish an order event with the .NET Event Grid SDK.
output_examples.output: A concise plan covering package selection, authentication, event schema choice, client creation, single-event publishing, and batch publishing considerations.

output_examples.input: Review my pull delivery consumer design.
output: Confirms the receive loop handles lock tokens.
Recommends acknowledgement only after successful processing.
Suggests release for retryable failures and rejection for terminal failures.

output_examples.input: Compare EventGridEvent and CloudEvent for a new integration.
output_examples.output: A decision summary explaining Azure-specific compatibility, CloudEvents interoperability, required fields, and when each schema is a better fit.

best_practices: Prefer managed identity or Microsoft Entra ID when the deployment supports it.
Use CloudEvents for new cross-platform integrations unless Azure-specific schema features are required.
Design consumers to be idempotent because event delivery can occur more than once.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode real access keys, topic endpoints, or SAS tokens in source code.
Do not acknowledge pulled events before the application finishes durable processing.
Do not send large payloads through Event Grid when a storage reference is more appropriate.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure Event Grid resources?
faq.answer: No. It provides .NET SDK guidance and examples, but resource creation remains outside the skill.

faq.question: Does it support CloudEvents?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes CloudEvent publishing, parsing, and Azure Functions trigger guidance.

faq.question: Can it help with Event Grid namespaces?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers sender and receiver clients for namespace pull delivery patterns.

faq.question: Which authentication method should I prefer?
faq.answer: Prefer Microsoft Entra ID or managed identity when possible. Use keys or SAS only when required.

faq.question: Does it include production reliability advice?
faq.answer: Yes. It discusses batching, idempotent handlers, partial failures, dead-letter handling, and failover patterns.

faq.question: Is this limited to Claude?
faq.answer: No. The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
